_meta.note: Session 2 of 7. Strand 1 (Fundamental Rights + Principles of Policy) populated with verified content from the Nov 2025 / 27th Amendment edition in project knowledge. Descriptions are paraphrased, not verbatim (copyright discipline — full text lives in constitution_baretext.json, out of v1 scope). Strands 2-5 remain empty shells for later sessions. Session 6 of 7 added historicalConstitutions (1956, 1962) as structural Exploration entries per the design log's reversal — not essays, not muted reference cards. Each structural card carries a lineageForward note connecting it to the current 1973 text. Governor-General->President and C-in-C->COAS title-change facts (verified in an earlier session) are reflected here with correct attribution. Strand 2 (State Machinery) built with 15 verified articles — President, Parliament composition, and the Judiciary/Federal Constitutional Court structure under the 27th Amendment, verified directly against the Nov 2025 PDF's actual current text (Articles 175B-175F, confirming old Art.184 is omitted and its jurisdiction moved to the FCC). Federal Government chapter (Arts 90-100, PM/Cabinet) deliberately NOT included this pass — flagged for a follow-up verification pass rather than stated from memory. This is a scoped selection of structurally important articles, not exhaustive coverage of Arts 41-212 the way Strand 1 covered its full chapter range. Strand 4 (Islamic Provisions) built complete — all 5 articles (227-231), fully verified against the PDF. Strand 5 (Emergency/Amendment/Schedule) built PARTIAL — 3 articles (232, 233, 238) verified with confidence; 234-237 (provincial-machinery-failure emergency, financial emergency, revocation, indemnity laws) and 239 (amendment Bill procedure detail) deliberately left for a follow-up pass rather than stated from incomplete verification. Strand 3 (Federalism, ~40+ articles spanning Arts 101-159) NOT started this pass — flagged as needing its own dedicated session given its size, same restraint discipline as every other choice this build has made. Strand 3 (Federalism) built with 9 verified articles — Governor/Provincial Government structure and, most importantly, Articles 141-144: the actual textual mechanism of the 18th Amendment's Concurrent List abolition, verified directly against the PDF (confirmed: 'Concurrent legislative list and the entries 1 to 47 omitted' in the Fourth Schedule). This directly grounds the already-written 18th Amendment essay in real article text rather than narrative summary. NOT included this pass: Council of Common Interests (Art.153), NFC (Art.160), and the full Provincial Assembly procedural chapter (Arts.106-127) — index-only, not verified with full text, left for a follow-up pass rather than guessed. Follow-up pass added 5 verified Federal Government articles (90-92, 95, 97) to Strand 2 — PM election mechanism, Cabinet size cap (11% of Parliament, 18th Amdt), and the actual no-confidence procedure (20% threshold, 3-7 day window) behind real events like the April 2022 vote against Imran Khan's government. State Machinery strand now at 21 articles. Follow-up pass added Council of Common Interests (153, 154) and National Finance Commission (160) to Federalism strand — verified complete text, including the NFC's 'ratchet' safeguard (Art.160(3A)) that grounds the 18th Amendment essay's 47.5%->57.5% claim in actual constitutional text rather than the essay's own narrative summary. Federalism strand now at 12 articles. Follow-up pass completed Strand 5 (Emergency/Amendment) — added 234, 235, 236, 237, 239, all fully verified. Article 239 is a genuinely important find: the 27th Amendment (2025) added clause (5), explicitly barring any court from ever questioning a constitutional amendment on any ground — making Pakistan's amendment power textually unlimited, unlike India's judicially-created 'basic structure doctrine' limit. Strand 5 now complete at 8 articles (232, 233, 234, 235, 236, 237, 238, 239). Cross-reference and lineage-trail expansion pass: added cross-refs to Art.227 (S.295-C PPC), Art.58 (historical 58(2)(b) dismissal-power history), Art.233 (verified real invocations in 1973/1974/1998, sourced from the Constitution's own footnoted record). Built 3 new full lineage trails beyond Article 9: Art.95 (no-confidence, tracing the 58(2)(b) detour and the 2022 Imran Khan vote), Art.175 (the full SC-to-FCC restructuring arc across the 18th/26th/27th Amendments), and Art.239 (the amendment procedure's own evolution, ending on the 27th Amendment's no-judicial-review clause). Trails now exist on 4 articles total (9, 95, 175, 239), not just the single worked example from Session 2.
_meta.schema: Each article: {num, title, justiciable(bool), description, crossRefs:[{code,note}], lineage:[{label,detail}]}
_meta.crossRefCaveat: Cross-references are seeded conservatively this pass — only pairings with clear, well-established doctrinal grounding are included. This list is a starting set, not exhaustive; expanding it further is real verification work for a later pass, not guessed.
_meta.predecessorCaveat: Predecessor-constitution mapping (1956/1962 lineage) is NOT included in this pass. Per the design log's own caveat, promising accurate article-by-article mapping across three constitutions without dedicated verification work is a real accuracy risk — deferred rather than guessed.

strands.label: Strand 1
strands.name: Fundamental Rights
strands.description: Chapter 1, Part II — justiciable. Courts void any law inconsistent with this Chapter (Art. 8).

articles.title: Laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights to be void
articles.description: Any law, or custom having the force of law, that conflicts with this Chapter is void to the extent of that conflict. The State cannot make a law that takes away or narrows these rights.

articles.title: Security of person
articles.description: No person shall be deprived of life or liberty save in accordance with law.

crossRefs.note: capital punishment debates

lineage.label: 1973 original text
lineage.detail: Article 9 alone — narrow textual guarantee of life and liberty.

lineage.label: Shehla Zia v. WAPDA, PLD 1994 SC 693
lineage.detail: Read with Article 14 (dignity) to imply a right to a healthy environment, decades before any standalone textual right existed.

lineage.label: 26th Amendment, 2024
lineage.detail: Article 9-A inserted — the environmental right becomes its own standalone justiciable text rather than an interpretive stretch.

articles.title: Clean and healthy environment
articles.description: Every person has the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment. Inserted by the 26th Amendment, 2024 — codifying what Shehla Zia v. WAPDA had argued its way to through Article 9 three decades earlier.

crossRefs.note: see Landmark Cases

articles.title: Safeguards as to arrest and detention
articles.description: A person arrested must be informed of the grounds for arrest, given the right to consult and be defended by a lawyer of their choice, and produced before a magistrate within 24 hours (excluding travel time).

articles.title: Right to fair trial
articles.description: For determination of civil rights and obligations, or any criminal charge, a person is entitled to a fair trial and due process. Inserted by the 18th Amendment, 2010 — previously fair trial was pieced together from other Articles rather than stated outright.

articles.title: Slavery, forced labour, etc., prohibited
articles.description: Slavery is non-existent and forbidden. All forced labour and human trafficking are prohibited. No child under 14 may be engaged in a factory, mine, or other hazardous employment.

articles.title: Protection against retrospective punishment
articles.description: No law can punish an act that was not a crime when committed, or impose a harsher penalty than existed at the time of the offence.

articles.title: Protection against double punishment and self-incrimination
articles.description: No person shall be prosecuted or punished twice for the same offence, or compelled to be a witness against themselves.

articles.title: Inviolability of dignity of man
articles.description: The dignity of man, and subject to law, the privacy of the home, are inviolable. No person may be tortured for the purpose of extracting evidence.

crossRefs.note: read with Art. 9 — see Landmark Cases

articles.title: Freedom of movement
articles.description: Every citizen may remain in, enter, and move freely throughout Pakistan, and reside and settle anywhere in it, subject to reasonable restriction by law in the public interest.

articles.title: Freedom of assembly
articles.description: Every citizen may assemble peacefully and without arms, subject to reasonable restrictions imposed by law in the interest of public order.

articles.title: Freedom of association
articles.description: Every citizen has the right to form associations and unions, subject to reasonable restrictions by law in the interest of sovereignty, integrity, public order, or morality.

articles.title: Freedom of trade, business or profession
articles.description: Every citizen may enter any lawful profession or trade and conduct any lawful business, subject to qualification requirements and regulation.

articles.title: Freedom of speech
articles.description: Every citizen has the right to freedom of speech and expression, subject to reasonable restrictions in the interest of the glory of Islam, the security of Pakistan, friendly foreign relations, public order, decency, morality, contempt of court, or incitement to an offence.

crossRefs.note: blasphemy — well-established tension point

articles.title: Right to information
articles.description: Every citizen has the right to have access to information in matters of public importance, subject to regulation by law. Inserted by the 18th Amendment, 2010.

articles.title: Freedom to profess religion
articles.description: Every citizen has the right to profess, practise, and propagate their religion, and every religious denomination has the right to establish, maintain, and manage its own religious institutions.

crossRefs.note: tension with Art. 2A / Art. 227-231 — see Turtles

articles.title: Safeguard against religious taxation
articles.description: No person shall be compelled to pay a tax whose proceeds are specifically spent on the propagation of any religion other than their own.

articles.title: Safeguards as to educational institutions
articles.description: No person attending an educational institution may be required to receive religious instruction relating to a religion other than their own, or to attend religious worship other than their own.

articles.title: Provision as to property
articles.description: Every citizen has the right to acquire, hold, and dispose of property in any part of Pakistan, subject to law and the public interest.

articles.title: Protection of property rights
articles.description: No person shall be deprived of property except in accordance with law; compulsory acquisition or requisition requires law providing for compensation.

articles.title: Equality of citizens
articles.description: All citizens are equal before law and entitled to equal protection of law. There shall be no discrimination on the basis of sex alone. Nothing prevents the State from making special provision for the protection of women and children.

crossRefs.note: rape law reform debates

articles.title: Right to education
articles.description: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children aged 5 to 16, in a manner determined by law. Inserted by the 18th Amendment, 2010.

articles.title: Non-discrimination in access to public places
articles.description: No citizen shall be denied access to public places (subject to their special nature) on grounds only of race, religion, caste, sex, residence, or place of birth.

articles.title: Safeguard against discrimination in services
articles.description: No citizen otherwise qualified for appointment to a Pakistan service shall be discriminated against on grounds only of race, religion, caste, sex, residence, or place of birth.

articles.title: Preservation of language, script and culture
articles.description: Any section of citizens with a distinct language, script, or culture has the right to preserve and promote it, and to establish institutions for that purpose.

strands.label: Strand 1
strands.name: Principles of Policy
strands.description: Chapter 2, Part II — non-justiciable. Courts cannot strike down legislation for falling short of these Principles (Art. 30); they guide policy, not litigation.

articles.title: Principles of Policy
articles.description: Every organ and authority of the State is responsible for acting in accordance with these Principles, subject to available resources. An annual report on their observance must be laid before Parliament / the Provincial Assembly.

articles.title: Responsibility with respect to Principles of Policy
articles.description: Whether an action complies with the Principles of Policy is for the relevant organ or authority itself to decide — the validity of a law or action cannot be challenged in court on the ground that it doesn't follow these Principles. This is the clause that makes Chapter 2 non-justiciable — see the Turtles essay on the non-justiciability trap.

articles.title: Islamic way of life
articles.description: Steps shall be taken to enable Muslims to order their lives according to Islam, including compulsory Quran teaching, encouraging Arabic learning, and proper organisation of zakat and mosques.

articles.title: Promotion of local Government institutions
articles.description: The State shall encourage local government institutions with elected representatives, giving special representation to peasants, workers, and women.

articles.title: Parochial prejudices to be discouraged
articles.description: The State shall discourage parochial, racial, tribal, sectarian, and provincial prejudices among citizens.

articles.title: Full participation of women in national life
articles.description: Steps shall be taken to ensure full participation of women in all spheres of national life.

articles.title: Protection of family
articles.description: The State shall protect marriage, the family, the mother, and the child.

articles.title: Protection of minorities
articles.description: The State shall safeguard the legitimate rights and interests of minorities, including their due representation in Federal and Provincial services.

articles.title: Promotion of social justice
articles.description: The State shall promote the educational and economic interests of backward classes, remove illiteracy, ensure inexpensive justice, prevent exploitative practices, and decentralise government administration.

articles.title: Promotion of social and economic well-being
articles.description: The State shall secure well-being irrespective of sex, caste, creed or race — preventing wealth concentration, providing social security, basic necessities, and reducing income disparity. Includes the mandate to eliminate riba (interest) before 1 January 2028.

articles.title: Participation of people in Armed Forces
articles.description: The State shall enable people from all parts of Pakistan to participate in the Armed Forces.

articles.title: Strengthening bonds with the Muslim world
articles.description: The State shall endeavour to preserve and strengthen fraternal relations among Muslim countries based on Islamic unity, support the common interests of the peoples of Asia, Africa and Latin America, promote international peace and security, and foster goodwill among all nations.

strands.label: Strand 2
strands.name: State Machinery
strands.description: Federal legislature, executive, judiciary (incl. Federal Constitutional Court, 27th Amendment).

articles.title: The President
articles.description: There shall be a President of Pakistan, Head of State, representing the unity of the Republic — must be a Muslim, 45+, qualified for National Assembly membership. Elected by an electoral college of both Houses of Parliament plus the Provincial Assemblies, not by direct popular vote. The validity of a presidential election cannot be questioned in any court.

articles.title: Term of office of President
articles.description: Five-year term, eligible for re-election but capped at two consecutive terms. Continues in office until a successor is sworn in even after the term expires — no gap in headship of state.

articles.title: President's power to grant pardon
articles.description: The President may grant pardon, reprieve, and respite, and remit, suspend, or commute any sentence passed by any court or tribunal.

articles.title: Removal or impeachment of President
articles.description: The President may be removed for physical/mental incapacity or impeached for violating the Constitution or gross misconduct. Requires a notice signed by half the total membership of either House, a joint sitting within 7-14 days, and a two-thirds vote of total Parliament membership to actually remove.

articles.title: President to act on advice
articles.description: The President acts on and in accordance with Cabinet or Prime Ministerial advice — may ask for reconsideration once, but must act on the advice given after reconsideration. Acts in personal discretion only where the Constitution explicitly empowers it, and that discretionary use cannot be questioned on any ground. What advice was actually given cannot be inquired into by any court.

crossRefs.note: the discretionary National Assembly dissolution power sits inside this framework

articles.title: Majlis-e-Shoora (Parliament)
articles.description: Parliament consists of the President and two Houses — the National Assembly and the Senate. Establishes the bicameral structure that distinguishes 1973 from both 1956 and 1962 (see the historical Exploration entries).

articles.title: Dissolution of the National Assembly
articles.description: The President dissolves the National Assembly if advised by the Prime Minister — dissolution follows automatically 48 hours after such advice. A narrow discretionary dissolution power also exists where a no-confidence vote has passed and no member commands majority confidence. A caretaker Cabinet is appointed and a general election held within 90 days.

crossRefs.note: the President's unilateral dissolution power — inserted by the 8th Amdt (1985), used to dismiss 4 governments 1988-96, finally repealed by the 18th Amdt (2010). Current Art.58 shown here is the post-2010 text.

articles.title: The Senate
articles.description: 96 members: 14 elected by each Provincial Assembly, plus seats for the Federal Capital, women, technocrats (including ulema) elected by each Provincial Assembly. Senate seats are explicitly NOT population-proportional — this is the direct institutional answer to smaller provinces' fear of Punjab dominating a single house (see the 18th Amendment/Federalism essay and the 1956/1962 historical comparison).

articles.title: Disqualification on grounds of defection
articles.description: A member who defects — votes against party lines on a no-confidence motion, money bill, or constitutional amendment, or resigns/joins another party — can be declared to have vacated their seat by the party head, subject to Election Commission and court review. The anti-defection clause, aimed at preventing horse-trading in no-confidence votes.

articles.title: Establishment and jurisdiction of courts
articles.description: As amended by the 27th Amendment (2025): there shall be a Federal Constitutional Court, a Supreme Court, a High Court for each Province and for Islamabad, and such other courts as law establishes. No court has jurisdiction except what the Constitution or law confers. The Judiciary is to be progressively separated from the Executive.

lineage.label: 1973 original text
lineage.detail: Established a Supreme Court and High Courts only — a single apex court structure.

lineage.label: 18th Amendment, 2010
lineage.detail: Article 175A inserted — created the Judicial Commission of Pakistan for judicial appointments, moving the process away from unilateral executive discretion.

lineage.label: 26th Amendment, 2024
lineage.detail: 175A restructured — JCP composition changed to include more parliamentary and executive representation, reducing the judiciary's own majority within its appointing body.

lineage.label: 27th Amendment, 2025
lineage.detail: New Chapter 1A (Articles 175B-175L) inserted — creates the Federal Constitutional Court as a wholly separate apex body. Old Article 184 (Supreme Court's fundamental-rights original jurisdiction) formally omitted; that power moves to the FCC via new Article 175E. The Supreme Court's role narrows to ordinary appellate work under Article 185.

articles.title: Appointment of Judges
articles.description: Governs appointment of judges to the Federal Constitutional Court, Supreme Court, High Courts, and Federal Shariat Court, via the Judicial Commission of Pakistan — the mechanism created by the 18th Amendment (2010) and substantially restructured by the 26th (2024) and 27th (2025) Amendments to reduce the judiciary's own majority within the appointing body. See the Judicial Review Turtles essay for the doctrinal history this sits inside.

articles.title: Constitution of Federal Constitutional Court
articles.description: New Chapter 1A, inserted by the 27th Amendment (2025). The Federal Constitutional Court must have an equal number of judges from each Province and at least one from the Islamabad High Court, with that Islamabad-seat number never exceeding any single province's.

articles.title: Appointment of Federal Constitutional Court Judges
articles.description: Judges must be citizens of Pakistan who are or have been a Supreme Court judge, or have served 5+ years as a High Court judge, or 20+ years as a High Court advocate who is also a Supreme Court advocate.

articles.title: Original jurisdiction of Federal Constitutional Court
articles.description: The FCC has EXCLUSIVE original jurisdiction over disputes between two or more governments (Federal or Provincial) — declaratory judgments only. Critically, it also now holds the fundamental-rights public-importance jurisdiction that Article 184(3) gave the Supreme Court before the 27th Amendment — that old Article 184 is now formally omitted. All pending cases under the old provision were transferred to the FCC automatically on the Amendment's commencement. This is the article that resolves the 'Writ Jurisdiction Reference' open item flagged earlier in this project — the FCC, not the Supreme Court, is now the body to reference for constitutional/fundamental-rights writ jurisdiction.

crossRefs.note: now omitted — jurisdiction moved to FCC

articles.title: Appellate jurisdiction of the Federal Constitutional Court
articles.description: The FCC hears appeals from High Court judgments where an Act of Parliament so provides, where the High Court certifies a substantial constitutional-interpretation question, or (with the FCC's leave) from a High Court's Article 199 writ order — with rent/family-matter exceptions.

articles.title: Appellate jurisdiction of the Supreme Court
articles.description: Post-27th Amendment, this is where the Supreme Court's remaining role actually sits: ordinary civil and criminal appeals from High Courts — death-sentence reversals on appeal, contempt punishments, and disputes above a set monetary threshold. Old Articles 184 (original jurisdiction) and 186/186A (advisory jurisdiction, case-transfer power) are now omitted — this is the textual confirmation of the Supreme Court's narrowed role discussed in the Judicial Review essay.

articles.title: The Federal Government
articles.description: Federal executive authority is exercised in the President's name by the Federal Government — the Prime Minister and Federal Ministers — acting through the Prime Minister, who is the Federation's chief executive. This is the article that actually locates real executive power: the President reigns in name (Art. 99), the Prime Minister governs.

articles.title: The Cabinet
articles.description: The National Assembly, after electing its Speaker and Deputy Speaker, must — to the exclusion of all other business — elect one of its Muslim members as Prime Minister by majority of total membership (a runoff between the top two if no majority emerges on the first poll). No term limit on how many times someone may hold the office. The Cabinet is collectively responsible to Parliament, and the Prime Minister holds office at the President's pleasure — but the President may only act on that pleasure if satisfied the PM has lost the confidence of the Assembly majority, and even then must first require a confidence vote rather than dismiss outright.

articles.title: Federal Ministers and Ministers of State
articles.description: The President appoints Federal Ministers and Ministers of State from Parliament's membership on the Prime Minister's advice. No more than one-quarter of Federal Ministers may come from the Senate rather than the National Assembly, and the 18th Amendment capped the total Cabinet (including Ministers of State) at 11% of Parliament's total membership — a direct check on cabinet bloat.

articles.title: Vote of no-confidence against Prime Minister
articles.description: A no-confidence resolution needs at least 20% of the National Assembly's total membership to move it, cannot be voted on before 3 days or after 7 days from being moved, cannot be moved while the Assembly is considering the Annual Budget, and removes the Prime Minister immediately if passed by a majority of total membership. The procedural mechanism behind every high-profile no-confidence vote in Pakistani politics — including the one that removed Imran Khan's government in April 2022.

lineage.label: 1973 original text
lineage.detail: No-confidence procedure existed from the outset, requiring the same 20%-to-move / majority-to-pass structure.

lineage.label: 8th Amendment, 1985
lineage.detail: Article 58(2)(b) gave the President an independent, competing power to dismiss a PM/dissolve the Assembly outright — bypassing this Article's parliamentary mechanism entirely.

lineage.label: 18th Amendment, 2010
lineage.detail: 58(2)(b) repealed. This Article's parliamentary no-confidence procedure became, once again, the only constitutional route to remove a sitting Prime Minister.

lineage.label: April 2022
lineage.detail: Used against Imran Khan's government — the most prominent real-world application of this exact clause.

articles.title: Extent of executive authority of the Federation
articles.description: Federal executive authority extends to whatever matters Parliament has power to legislate on — including exercising rights and jurisdiction outside Pakistan itself. Where a Provincial Assembly also has legislative power over a matter, federal executive authority does not automatically extend into the province on that matter unless the Constitution or a Parliamentary law expressly says so — a structural echo of the Art.142/143 federalism boundary in Strand 3.

crossRefs.note: see Federalism strand — same boundary logic, executive instead of legislative

strands.label: Strand 3
strands.name: Federalism
strands.description: Provinces, Governor, Provincial Assembly, Federal/Concurrent Legislative Lists.

articles.title: Appointment of Governor
articles.description: A Governor for each Province, appointed by the President on the Prime Minister's advice — not elected. Must be qualified for National Assembly membership, 35+, and (per the 18th Amendment) a registered voter resident of that specific Province — closing an earlier loophole that let the Federal Government post an outsider to govern a province.

articles.title: The Provincial Government
articles.description: Provincial executive authority is exercised in the Governor's name by the Provincial Government — Chief Minister and Provincial Ministers — acting through the Chief Minister. Structurally mirrors the President/PM split at the federal level (Art. 48).

articles.title: The Provincial Cabinet
articles.description: A Cabinet of Ministers with the Chief Minister at its head advises the Governor, exercising real executive power the way the Federal Cabinet advises the President.

articles.title: Local Government
articles.description: Each Province must, by law, establish an elected local government system and devolve political, administrative, and financial authority to it — inserted by the 18th Amendment. This is the second-tier devolution the Federalism essay flagged as the honest asterisk on the 18th Amendment's legacy: provinces have been consistently slower to hand power down to this tier than they were to receive it from the Federation.

crossRefs.note: see Turtles — local-government devolution lag

articles.title: Extent of Federal and Provincial laws
articles.description: Parliament may legislate for the whole or any part of Pakistan (including extra-territorially); a Provincial Assembly may legislate for its Province.

articles.title: Subject-matter of Federal and Provincial laws
articles.description: Parliament has exclusive power over the Federal Legislative List. Both Parliament and Provincial Assemblies may legislate on criminal law, procedure, and evidence — genuinely concurrent, but narrower than the old Concurrent List. A Provincial Assembly — not Parliament — has power over anything NOT on the Federal Legislative List. This is the 18th Amendment's core textual mechanism: the old Concurrent List was deleted outright, and this residual clause is what turned 47 formerly-shared subjects into exclusively provincial ones overnight.

crossRefs.note: see Turtles — this is the actual clause that abolished the Concurrent List

articles.title: Inconsistency between Federal and Provincial laws
articles.description: Where a competent Act of Parliament conflicts with a Provincial Act, the federal law prevails and the provincial law is void to the extent of the conflict — regardless of which was passed first. The textual basis for the Sui Southern Gas reasoning discussed in the Federalism essay: a devolved subject can still be overridden by valid federal legislation where genuine inconsistency exists.

crossRefs.note: see 18th Amendment essay — labour law re-federalized via this clause

articles.title: Parliament legislating for provinces by consent
articles.description: If one or more Provincial Assemblies pass resolutions requesting it, Parliament may legislate on a matter otherwise outside its reach for those provinces specifically — and that province's own Assembly can later amend or repeal that law itself. An opt-in mechanism for provinces to borrow federal legislative capacity without permanently ceding the subject.

articles.title: Federation entrusting functions to Provinces
articles.description: The Federal Government may, with a Province's consent, entrust federal executive functions to that Province or its officers — with the Federation paying the Province's extra administrative costs, arbitrated by the Federal Constitutional Court's Chief Justice if the parties can't agree on the amount.

articles.title: Council of Common Interests
articles.description: The CCI: Prime Minister as chairman, all four provincial Chief Ministers, plus three federally-nominated members. Responsible to Parliament, must submit an Annual Report. This is the constitutional forum where federal-provincial disputes over shared-interest matters (electricity, natural resources) actually get negotiated, not litigated.

articles.title: CCI functions and rules of procedure
articles.description: Formulates and controls policy on Federal Legislative List Part II matters (the 18-entry list of genuinely shared federal-provincial concerns: electricity, major ports, national planning, inter-provincial coordination). Must be constituted within 30 days of a new PM's oath, meets at least every 90 days, decisions by majority vote. If the Federal or a Provincial Government disagrees with a CCI decision, the matter goes to a Parliamentary joint sitting for final resolution — not to court.

articles.title: National Finance Commission
articles.description: A Commission — federal and provincial Finance Ministers plus presidential appointees — reconstituted at least every 5 years, recommends how to split the divisible tax pool between the Federation and Provinces. Critically, an 18th Amendment safeguard now states a province's share in any new Award CANNOT be less than its share in the previous one — a ratchet that only moves toward greater provincial share, never back. This is the actual textual mechanism behind the 47.5%→57.5% shift the 18th Amendment essay describes.

crossRefs.note: see Turtles — the 3A ratchet clause behind the NFC share increase

strands.label: Strand 4
strands.name: Islamic Provisions
strands.description: Articles 227–231 — Council of Islamic Ideology, Islamisation of laws.

articles.title: Provisions relating to the Holy Quran and Sunnah
articles.description: All existing laws must conform to the Injunctions of Islam as laid down in the Quran and Sunnah, and no repugnant law may be enacted. For a Muslim sect's personal law, 'Quran and Sunnah' means as interpreted by that sect. Does NOT affect non-Muslim citizens' personal laws or their status as citizens — this carve-out is the textual anchor for the Article 2A/20/25 tension essay already written.

crossRefs.note: see Turtles: Article 2A, 20 & 25 tension essay
blasphemy — the sharpest real-world test of the Art.227(3) non-Muslim carve-out

articles.title: Composition of the Islamic Council
articles.description: The Council of Islamic Ideology — 8 to 20 members appointed by the President, drawn from Islamic scholarship and from those understanding Pakistan's economic/political/legal/administrative problems. At least 2 must be current or former superior court judges, at least one-third engaged 15+ years in Islamic research, at least one member a woman. Three-year terms.

articles.title: Reference to the Islamic Council
articles.description: The President, a Governor, or one-quarter of a House's/Assembly's membership may refer a proposed law to the Islamic Council for an opinion on whether it's repugnant to Islamic Injunctions.

articles.title: Functions of the Islamic Council
articles.description: Advisory only — recommends how laws can be brought into conformity with Islamic Injunctions, advises on referred questions, and reports to Parliament, which must then legislate on the final report within two years. A proposed law can be enacted before the Council's advice arrives if public interest requires — the advice does not have a veto, only a reconsideration trigger.

articles.title: Rules of procedure
articles.description: The Islamic Council regulates its own proceedings via rules approved by the President.

articles.title: Proclamation of emergency
articles.description: The President may proclaim emergency where Pakistan's security is threatened by war, external aggression, or internal disturbance beyond a Province's control to manage. Internal-disturbance emergencies require that Province's own Assembly to pass a resolution first — a 2010 (18th Amendment) safeguard against a unilateral federal declaration against a province's wishes. Any presidential proclamation must be approved by both Houses within 10 days. During emergency, Parliament can legislate for a Province on matters otherwise outside its reach, and the Federal Government can assume provincial executive functions.

articles.title: Power to suspend Fundamental Rights during emergency
articles.description: During a proclaimed emergency, the President may suspend the right to move any court to enforce specified Fundamental Rights, and orders taken under that suspension cannot be challenged in any court on the ground of inconsistency with those Rights. One of the sharpest tensions in the whole document — an explicit, textual override of the Chapter 1 justiciability guarantee this app treats as its central organizing badge.

crossRefs.note: actually invoked: 14 Aug 1973 (continued in force via joint-sitting resolutions through 1974) and 28 May 1998 — verified against the Constitution's own official footnoted record

articles.title: Amendment of the Constitution
articles.description: A constitutional amendment Bill requires a two-thirds majority of the total membership of each House separately. This is the mechanism behind all 27 amendments discussed throughout this module's essays — including the 26th and 27th Amendments restructuring the judiciary. No entrenched/unamendable clause exists in this Constitution the way Germany's Basic Law has one (see the First Principles postwar-wave essay) — everything, in principle, can be amended by this same two-thirds procedure.

crossRefs.note: compare Germany's unamendable 'eternity clause'

articles.title: Failure of constitutional machinery in a Province
articles.description: If the President, on the Governor's report, is satisfied a Province's government cannot be carried on per the Constitution, the President may (or must, if both Houses pass a resolution) proclaim and assume the Province's executive functions directly, or direct the Governor to do so on the Federation's behalf — bypassing the Provincial Assembly.

articles.title: Financial emergency
articles.description: Where the President is satisfied Pakistan's (or a Province's) economic life, financial stability, or credit is threatened, a Proclamation can direct provinces to observe specified financial propriety principles — including reducing salaries of provincial and (if needed) federal government employees.

articles.title: Revocation of Proclamation
articles.description: Any emergency Proclamation may be varied or revoked by a later one — and its validity cannot be questioned in any court. A second explicit ouster clause layered on top of Article 233's fundamental-rights suspension power.

crossRefs.note: the two provisions together — suspension power plus non-justiciability of the Proclamation itself

articles.title: Indemnity laws
articles.description: Parliament may pass laws indemnifying — legally protecting from consequence — anyone in federal or provincial government service, or any other person, for acts done in connection with maintaining or restoring order. The textual mechanism that has, historically, shielded officials acting under martial law or emergency from later prosecution.

articles.title: Constitution amendment Bill procedure
articles.description: An amendment Bill needs two-thirds of the total membership in each House separately (with a reconciliation process if amended by the second House), then presidential assent — and if it would alter a Province's boundaries, that Province's own Assembly must also approve by two-thirds. Amended by the 27th Amendment (2025) to add clause (5): no court has jurisdiction to question ANY constitutional amendment on any ground whatsoever — a direct, textual response to any future attempt to have a court strike down an amendment the way courts elsewhere sometimes review amendments for basic-structure violations. Clause (6) reinforces this: there is no limitation whatever on Parliament's amending power. Together, these make Pakistan's amendment power textually unlimited in a way India's 'basic structure doctrine' (Kesavananda Bharati) explicitly is not — worth building into a future comparative note.

crossRefs.note: s.50 — the no-judicial-review-of-amendments clause

lineage.label: 8th Amendment, 1985 (P.O. 14 of 1985)
lineage.detail: Substituted the current form of Article 239, formalizing the two-House reconciliation process.

lineage.label: 27th Amendment, 2025
lineage.detail: New clause (5) added — bars any court from questioning a constitutional amendment on any ground whatsoever. Clause (6) reinforces there is no limitation on Parliament's amending power. Makes the amendment power textually unlimited, unlike India's judicially-created 'basic structure doctrine.'

historicalConstitutions.name: The 1956 Constitution
historicalConstitutions.fate: Abrogated by President Iskander Mirza's martial law, 7 October 1958 — in force under two years.

cards.title: Unicameral National Assembly
cards.description: A single elected National Assembly, direct adult franchise, seats split equally between East and West Pakistan under the parity principle regardless of population.
cards.lineageForward: 1973 replaced this with a bicameral Parliament — see State Machinery strand. By then East Pakistan had seceded, so parity was no longer the problem bicameralism had to solve.

cards.title: Governor-General title retired — President created
cards.description: The 1956 Constitution converted Pakistan from a Dominion to the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, retiring the Governor-General title inherited from 1947 and creating the office of President as head of state. Iskander Mirza became the first President.
cards.lineageForward: The President title has been carried forward unchanged through 1962 and 1973 — this is the constitution that actually created it, not 1973.

cards.title: Direct adult franchise
cards.description: Members of the National Assembly were directly elected by adult citizens — no intermediary electoral college.
cards.lineageForward: 1962 abandoned this for indirect election through Basic Democrats (see the 1962 entry). 1973 restored direct franchise and has kept it since.

cards.title: One Unit: West Pakistan merged into a single bloc
cards.description: All four West Pakistan provinces (Punjab, Sindh, NWFP, Balochistan) were administratively merged into a single 'One Unit' in 1955, specifically so West Pakistan as one bloc could sit at legislative parity with East Pakistan, despite East Pakistan holding the actual population majority.
cards.lineageForward: One Unit was dissolved in 1970, before the 1973 Constitution was drafted. 1973's federalism instead answers a different fracture — smaller provinces' fear of Punjab dominating a single house — through Senate parity. See the 18th Amendment essay.

cards.title: A Fundamental Rights chapter existed, justiciable in principle
cards.description: The 1956 Constitution included an enforceable Fundamental Rights chapter, structurally the ancestor of the current Chapter 1 — though the specific text and numbering do not map cleanly onto today's Articles 8-28, and dedicated verification work is needed before claiming article-by-article lineage beyond this general structural continuity.
cards.lineageForward: See the predecessorCaveat in this file's _meta — precise article-to-article mapping is deferred, not guessed.

historicalConstitutions.name: The 1962 Constitution
historicalConstitutions.fate: Abrogated 1969, following Ayub Khan's resignation amid nationwide unrest and the transfer of power to General Yahya Khan.

cards.title: Unicameral National Assembly, indirectly elected
cards.description: A single National Assembly, but its members — and the President — were now chosen indirectly through the Basic Democrats rather than by direct adult vote.
cards.lineageForward: 1973 restored a directly elected legislature and made it bicameral. The indirect-election model was never revived.

cards.title: A strong, directly empowered Presidency
cards.description: Ayub Khan's system concentrated executive authority in the President far more than 1956's parliamentary model had, reflecting his explicit view that 1956's instability came from an electorate not yet ready for direct, unmediated suffrage.
cards.lineageForward: 1973 rebalanced toward a parliamentary system with a Prime Minister as chief executive — though the President-vs-Prime Minister balance of power has itself been renegotiated repeatedly since, via the 8th, 13th, 17th, and 18th Amendments.

cards.title: The Basic Democrats — indirect election via ~80,000 local representatives
cards.description: Roughly 80,000 Basic Democrats, themselves locally elected, formed an electoral college that chose both the President and the National Assembly. Ayub Khan framed this explicitly as a corrective to what he saw as premature direct suffrage.
cards.lineageForward: This is the one structural feature of any of the three constitutions with no lineage forward at all — 1973 rejected it outright and no subsequent amendment has revived indirect election for the National Assembly.

cards.title: One Unit continued
cards.description: The 1962 Constitution preserved the One Unit arrangement and the East-West parity structure inherited from 1956, without fundamentally rethinking it.
cards.lineageForward: One Unit's failure to hold — East Pakistan's alienation deepened rather than resolved — is a direct throughline to the 1971 secession and, from there, to why 1973 had to invent a wholly different federalism. See the Three Constitutions, One Argument essay.

cards.title: Rights initially omitted, then restored under pressure
cards.description: Ayub Khan's original 1962 text notably omitted a justiciable Fundamental Rights chapter entirely, reflecting his general skepticism of what he considered Western-style constitutionalism slowing effective governance. Sustained political pressure forced their reinsertion via amendment within about a year.
cards.lineageForward: That a Fundamental Rights chapter could be dropped and then had to be fought back in is itself informative — it shows the 1973 Constitution's insistence on justiciable rights from the outset (Article 8's void-for-inconsistency clause) was a direct, deliberate reaction against this specific 1962 experience, not just convention.
